goal: Add sequential job-chain orchestration so a supervisor can submit multiple goals and have each next job start automatically after the previous job finishes successfully.
tech_stack: Go 1.26, stdlib JSON/file persistence, Gorechera orchestrator service, MCP JSON-RPC server
summary: Introduce persisted chain state in the domain and state store, add orchestrator service APIs to create/load/list/advance chains, and wire chain progression into terminal job handling so chained jobs start one-by-one. Expose the feature through MCP tools for chain creation and status lookup, add focused tests for persistence, service advancement, and MCP behavior, and update architecture/status docs to reflect the new sequential workflow surface.
product_scope: Persist chains in the file-backed state store with save/load/list behavior parallel to jobs.
Add orchestrator chain lifecycle methods: `StartChain`, `advanceChain`, `GetChain`, and `ListChains`.
Start the first chained job immediately and record per-goal `JobID` and status transitions.
Advance to the next goal only when the active chained job reaches `done`.
Mark the chain terminal state correctly when the last goal completes or when an active chained job cannot continue successfully.
Expose `gorechera_start_chain` and `gorechera_chain_status` in the MCP server.
Add or extend automated tests covering chain persistence, service behavior, and MCP tool behavior.
Update docs describing the new chain model and MCP tools.
non_goals: General DAG dependencies, branching, retries, or conditional chain logic.
Changing evaluator-gate semantics for individual jobs.
Adding new HTTP routes or CLI commands unless required by existing tests or plumbing.
Cross-process distributed locking beyond the current file-store/single-service model.
Changing worker, reviewer, or tester role contracts outside what chaining needs.
proposed_steps: Inspect current job creation, terminal-status handling, and MCP tool patterns to place chain hooks without bypassing existing evaluator-gated completion.
Add `JobChain` and `ChainGoal` to `internal/domain/types.go`, and add minimal chain linkage metadata to job state only if needed to identify the owning chain cleanly from completion/failure paths.
Extend `internal/store/state_store.go` with `SaveChain`, `LoadChain`, and `ListChains`, storing chain JSON under a dedicated `chains` directory and preserving the existing atomic-write pattern.

Implement `StartChain` in `internal/orchestrator/service.go` to validate input, create a chain record with pending goals, start goal 0 as a normal job, mark that goal running with its `JobID`, and persist the updated chain.
Implement `GetChain` and `ListChains` as thin service wrappers over the state store.

Implement `advanceChain` so it loads the current chain state, marks the completed goal done, increments `CurrentIndex`, starts the next job when one remains, or marks the chain done when the final goal finishes.

Wire chain progression into job terminal handling after evaluator-approved `done`, and also update chain state when a chained job ends blocked/failed so the chain does not remain incorrectly `running`.
Add MCP tool definitions, dispatch cases, argument parsing, and result serialization for `gorechera_start_chain` and `gorechera_chain_status` in `internal/mcp/server.go`.
Add tests for state-store chain persistence, service-level sequential advancement and stop-on-failure behavior, and MCP start/status tool responses.
Update `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` and `docs/IMPLEMENTATION_STATUS.md` to document chain persistence, orchestrator flow changes, and MCP tool availability.
Run `go build ./...` and `go test ./...` and use failures to tighten any missing plumbing or schema mismatches.
success_signals: A newly started chain shows goal 0 as `running` with a created job ID and all later goals as `pending`.
After goal 0's job reaches `done`, chain status shows goal 0 as `done` and goal 1 as `running` with a new job ID.
A completed multi-goal chain ends with chain status `done` and every goal status `done`.
A failing chained job leaves the chain in status `failed` with no subsequent goal started.
A chain JSON file exists under the state store's chain directory and can be reloaded after service restart.
MCP callers can create a chain and poll its status without needing job-level manual advancement.
Repository verification remains green with `go build ./...` and `go test ./...`.

verification_contract.goal: Verify that sequential job chaining is persisted correctly, advances only after evaluator-approved job completion, stops on unsuccessful terminal states, and is exposed through MCP tooling without regressing existing build/test behavior.
scope: Domain types for chain state in `internal/domain/types.go`.
Chain lifecycle and advancement wiring in `internal/orchestrator/service.go`.
MCP tool definitions and handlers in `internal/mcp/server.go`.
Documentation updates for architecture and implementation status.
required_checks: Creating a chain persists a chain file and immediately starts exactly the first job.
Chain status records `JobID` on each started goal and keeps later goals `pending` until advanced.
A `done` terminal job automatically advances the chain to the next goal.
A final `done` goal marks the whole chain `done`.
A `failed` or `blocked` chained job marks the chain `failed` and does not auto-start the next goal.
MCP `gorechera_start_chain` returns a chain identifier and MCP `gorechera_chain_status` returns the full persisted chain state.
Existing job start/status behavior still works and full repo build/tests pass.
disallowed_actions: Do not bypass `evaluateCompletion()` before marking a job `done`.
Do not pass full inter-agent conversation logs between jobs; preserve existing artifact/summary semantics.
Do not introduce executor-driven worker spawning or parallel chain execution.
Do not weaken approval handling for system actions.
Do not mix global and job-scoped harness ownership semantics.
Do not add Windows-specific core logic for chaining.
verification_contract.notes: Inference required: chain state needs a reliable way to associate a completed job back to its chain. The implementation may either add minimal chain metadata to `domain.Job` or perform a persisted chain lookup by `JobID`, but the chosen approach must preserve current job semantics and keep advancement deterministic.
